title: AMD Stretches Server DRAM With Flash Extended Memory
gist: AMD acquired startup MEXT, whose AI-driven 'Predictive Memory Engine' transparently extends DRAM with flash at the page level, letting servers hit 1.7X better price/performance versus pure-DRAM configs — a direct response to a DRAM pricing crisis so severe that Dell has stopped offering 3TB/6TB memory options on its PowerEdge R6725 servers.

themes.theme: The DRAM pricing crisis reshaping server economics

insights.claim: DRAM main memory cost as a share of total server bill-of-materials has risen from about 50% in 2023 (versus ~25% for CPUs) to between 60% and 90% by mid-2026, averaging around 75%, even though CPU prices themselves have also risen.
insights.speaker: Timothy Prickett Morgan
insights.so_what: Server TCO models built around historical CPU-dominated cost structures are now badly out of date; any hardware or software approach that reduces DRAM footprint (like flash-extended memory or CXL pooling) has an outsized effect on total system cost today.
insights.quote: DRAM main memory has gone from representing around 50 percent of the cost of a server, with CPUs being about a quarter of the bill of materials in 2023 ... to DRAM being somewhere between 60 percent and 90 percent of the cost of a server here in the middle of 2026, averaging around 75 percent.

insights.claim: According to Counterpoint Research, 64GB DIMM memory prices rose 3.5X between Q3 2025 and Q1 2026, and are on track to be up roughly 5X by Q3 2026, with Micron's own financials showing no end to the demand shock through 2028.
insights.speaker: Timothy Prickett Morgan
insights.so_what: A multi-year DRAM price supercycle (through 2028) implies memory-heavy workloads (databases, in-memory analytics) will see sustained cost inflation, favoring vendors offering memory-substitution technology and pressuring hyperscaler capex plans.
insights.quote: 64 GB DIMM memory prices rose by a factor of 3.5X between Q3 2025 and Q1 2026, and it sure looks like they will be up by a factor of 5X by Q3 2026.

insights.claim: Memory makers (Micron, Samsung, SK Hynix) are reallocating DRAM production toward higher-margin HBM stacked memory for AI accelerators, and cutting flash output to boost DRAM supply, while total DRAM and flash production capacity only grows 20-25% per year against demand growing far faster.
insights.speaker: Timothy Prickett Morgan
insights.so_what: Structural supply reallocation toward HBM (not just cyclical shortage) means conventional server DRAM scarcity is a durable side effect of the AI buildout, not a temporary blip — memory makers are 'getting filthy rich' as capacity discipline persists.
insights.quote: the three big memory makers are allocating more and more of their DRAM production to pricey HBM stacked memory, which is cutting back on normal DRAM chips used in server DIMM modules, and they are also cutting back on flash output to try to boost DRAM.
insights.locator: And now, the three big memory makers

insights.claim: Dell had stopped offering 3TB and 6TB main memory configuration options on its PowerEdge R6725 servers as of February 1, 2026, illustrating that DRAM scarcity is already constraining OEM product configurations, not just raising prices.
insights.speaker: Timothy Prickett Morgan
insights.so_what: When a major OEM removes high-memory SKUs entirely rather than just raising price, it signals genuine allocation/supply constraints rather than pure margin opportunism — a leading indicator to watch across other server vendors' configurators.
insights.quote: Dell is no longer shipping PowerEdge R6725 servers with 3 TB and 6 TB options for main memory. (This was as of February 1.)

themes.theme: AMD's MEXT acquisition and the flash-as-memory bet

insights.claim: AMD acquired startup MEXT (founded 2023, exited stealth in April 2026) for an undisclosed sum to get its 'Predictive Memory Engine,' which transparently extends DRAM capacity using flash storage with no hardware or software changes required to existing systems.
insights.speaker: Timothy Prickett Morgan
insights.so_what: AMD is directly productizing a software/firmware-level answer to the DRAM cost crisis, potentially giving it a differentiated platform-level TCO pitch versus Intel Xeon systems that lack an equivalent memory-extension technology today.
insights.quote: AMD just shelled out an undisclosed amount of money to acquire a shiny new startup called MEXT
insights.locator: This is why AMD just shelled out

insights.claim: MEXT's leadership pedigree traces to Fusion-io (early flash-as-memory commercialization with Apple and Meta as anchor customers), LSI Logic, and TidalScale (a HyperKernel hypervisor company acquired by HPE in December 2022 for $70.3 million raised); its chief scientist Carl Waldspurger previously architected VMware's DRS and NUMA/memory scheduling for ESX.
insights.speaker: Timothy Prickett Morgan
insights.so_what: The team's deep, decades-long lineage in memory-virtualization and flash-as-memory technology (going back to 1980s DOS extended-memory work) suggests MEXT's approach is technically credible rather than a marketing-driven repackaging of old ideas, raising the odds AMD's bet pays off commercially.
insights.quote: Carl Waldspurger, who was the principal engineer at VMware in charge of processor scheduling, memory management, and NUMA scheduling for the ESX hypervisor as well as the architect for VMware's Distributed Resource Scheduler, was tapped to be MEXT’s chief scientist.
insights.locator: Many of the 39 employees of MEXT

insights.claim: Flash is roughly 50X (possibly now 100X) cheaper per bit than DRAM and uses 30X less power per bit, but is about 500X slower — a gap MEXT's founder says required years of work to bridge via predictive prefetching rather than simple tiering.
insights.speaker: Gary Smerdon (quoted by Timothy Prickett Morgan)
insights.so_what: The 50-100X cost gap versus 'only' 500X latency gap is the core economic argument for flash-extended memory; any investor evaluating memory-tiering vendors should benchmark claims against this cost/latency ratio as the baseline case for viability.
insights.quote: it is still 50X less expensive than DRAM, with 30X lower power consumption as well. However, flash is also 500X slower than DRAM.
insights.locator: So extending memory is not a new idea

insights.claim: MEXT's core technical innovation, 'Predictive Memory,' uses an ensemble of AI models (including techniques inspired by LSTMs and LLM transformers used for sequence prediction) to forecast which memory pages will go from cold to hot and prefetch them from flash back into DRAM before they're needed, rather than relying on traditional reactive swap mechanisms.
insights.speaker: Carl Waldspurger (quoted by Timothy Prickett Morgan)
insights.so_what: This is a direct transfer of LLM-era sequence-modeling techniques into systems software (memory management), illustrating a broader pattern of AI-native techniques diffusing into traditionally non-AI infrastructure layers — a theme investors should watch for in other systems-software categories (storage tiering, caching, scheduling).
insights.quote: We were inspired by modern AI techniques based on neural networks like LSTMs and LLM transformers, which are actually really excellent at sequence prediction. Instead of predicting tokens in a natural language conversation, we are applying similar ideas to predict sequences of future memory page accesses.
insights.locator: We have developed sophisticated machine learning models

insights.claim: MEXT's recommended configuration is to provision half of needed memory capacity as real DRAM and half as flash, though the company has tested and validated a 25% DRAM / 75% flash ratio as workable, with performance tradeoffs presumed to be workload-dependent.
insights.speaker: Gary Smerdon (quoted by Timothy Prickett Morgan)
insights.so_what: A validated 1:3 DRAM-to-flash ratio (not just 1:1) means the addressable cost savings could be much larger than a simple 2X memory-cost reduction, if workloads tolerate the performance hit — a key variable for TCO modeling of MEXT-equipped servers.
insights.quote: Smerdon says that MEXT has tested systems with 25 percent DRAM and 75 percent flash and this works as well.
insights.locator: The recommended configuration for the MEXT memory expansion

insights.claim: In tests on an AMD Zen 5 128-core Epyc 9755 server, a 32GB DRAM + 32GB flash extended-memory configuration running Redis, Memcached, and Neo4j delivered 1.7X the price/performance of a 64GB pure-DRAM configuration while achieving roughly 95% of the throughput.
insights.speaker: Timothy Prickett Morgan
insights.so_what: A 1.7X price/performance gain with only a 5% throughput sacrifice is a compelling TCO argument for hyperscalers and enterprises running in-memory key/value stores and graph databases, and a template metric to benchmark competing memory-tiering solutions against.
insights.quote: the extended memory configuration with 32 GB of memory plus 32 GB of flash allocated to memory all did delivered 1.7X the bang for the buck of the machine with 64 GB of real DRAM allocated running the same workload.
insights.locator: MEXT has done some performance tests

insights.claim: In-memory databases, EDA, data analytics, digital content creation, and graph databases are described as strong fits for flash-extended memory, while traditional relational databases are a weaker fit; large banks and hedge funds are already customers, and graph database performance surprised MEXT's own team.
insights.speaker: Gary Smerdon (quoted by Timothy Prickett Morgan)
insights.so_what: Workload-selection matters for adoption: vendors and buyers should target EDA, analytics, content creation, and graph-database use cases first for flash-extended memory ROI, while being cautious applying it to relational database workloads.
insights.quote: Electronic design automation, data analytics, and digital content creation workloads “are screamingly good fits” for this extended memory, according to Smerdon.

insights.claim: In a Linux swap benchmark using Houdini on a 64-core Threadripper Pro 5995WX, a system cut to 16GB of real DRAM took 22,000 seconds to complete a swap-heavy workload without MEXT, versus 5,000 seconds with MEXT extended memory added — a 4.4X improvement — versus only a 1.3X improvement at the 32GB DRAM tier.
insights.speaker: Timothy Prickett Morgan
insights.so_what: The benefit of predictive memory extension scales sharply as real DRAM becomes scarcer relative to workload needs, meaning the technology's value proposition strengthens precisely as the DRAM shortage worsens — a self-reinforcing tailwind for MEXT/AMD adoption.
insights.quote: If you cut the machine back to 16 GB of real DRAM, the swap takes 22,000 seconds, but with MEXT extended memory with 16 GB of real DRAM plus flash, the swap time is reduced to 5,000 seconds. That is 1.3X better performance on a 32 GB DRAM configuration and a 4.4X better bang on a 16 GB DRAM configuration
insights.locator: Separately, MEXT did a Linux swap benchmark test

insights.claim: Before AMD's acquisition, MEXT charged customers a flat subscription fee of $3.99 per GB per year for its Predictive Memory Engine software; AMD has not disclosed its post-acquisition pricing or licensing plans.
insights.speaker: Timothy Prickett Morgan
insights.so_what: The pre-acquisition subscription price point gives a baseline for estimating potential AMD software-attach revenue per server if it commercializes MEXT broadly across its EPYC lineup, though AMD may bundle it rather than charge separately.
insights.quote: Before the AMD acquisition, MEXT was charging a flat fee for a subscription to its Predictive Memory Engine at $3.99 per GB per year.

themes.theme: Historical precedent and the open-platform question

insights.claim: The author frames MEXT's approach as finally realizing the promise Intel and Micron's 3D XPoint (launched July 2015) failed to deliver: memory-like speed at flash-like cost; Intel is blamed for under-investing in ramping 3D XPoint and then restricting it to its own Xeon platforms before killing the product line and selling its flash business to SK Hynix.
insights.speaker: Timothy Prickett Morgan
insights.so_what: Intel's decision to keep 3D XPoint proprietary rather than open it to the broader x86/Arm ecosystem is cited as a strategic failure that cost it a potentially lucrative, differentiated product line — a cautionary precedent AMD is being watched against with MEXT.
insights.quote: Had Intel not decided to try to keep 3D XPoint as something just for its own Xeon server processors, it may have gone mainstream and helped solve the current memory crisis. But alas, Intel lost its mind a little bit and killed off 3D XPoint
insights.locator: The promise of 3D XPoint was to have something

insights.claim: The author explicitly raises the open question of whether AMD will restrict MEXT's Predictive Memory Engine to its own EPYC processors and lock out Intel Xeon or homegrown Arm CPU platforms, repeating Intel's 3D XPoint mistake, but predicts AMD is 'highly likely' to take a more open approach.
insights.speaker: Timothy Prickett Morgan
insights.so_what: If AMD keeps MEXT exclusive to EPYC, it becomes a meaningful competitive moat against Intel Xeon on memory-cost TCO; if AMD opens it cross-platform, it becomes an industry-wide utility and a smaller direct AMD advantage — a key strategic decision point to monitor as AMD integrates the acquisition.
insights.quote: whether it will pull a 3D XPoint and not allow it on machines using Intel processors or those running homegrown Arm CPUs. Hopefully, AMD has a more open mind about this than Intel did. We think that is highly likely, in fact.
insights.locator: AMD has not disclosed what it paid
